From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

1585 treaty between England and the Dutch rebels

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Treaty_of_Nonsuch.pdf/page1-250px-Treaty_of_Nonsuch.pdf.jpg)](/wiki/File:Treaty_of_Nonsuch.pdf)

1732 copy of the text of the Treaty of Nonsuch (click for full PDF)

The **Treaty of Nonsuch** was signed on 10 August 1585 by [Elizabeth I of England](/wiki/Elizabeth_I_of_England "Elizabeth I of England") and the [Dutch rebels](/wiki/Dutch_Revolt "Dutch Revolt") fighting against Spanish rule. It was the first international treaty signed by what would become the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic"). It was signed at [Nonsuch Palace](/wiki/Nonsuch_Palace "Nonsuch Palace"), England.[[1]](#cite_note-Wernham-1)

## Terms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Treaty_of_Nonsuch&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terms")]

The treaty was provoked by the signing of the [Treaty of Joinville](/wiki/Treaty_of_Joinville "Treaty of Joinville") in 1584 between [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain") and the [Catholic League](/wiki/Catholic_League_(French) "Catholic League (French)") in which Philip II promised to finance the League.

Elizabeth I agreed to supply 6,400 foot soldiers and 1,000 cavalry (who were to be led by [Robert Dudley, the 1st Earl of Leicester](/wiki/Robert_Dudley,_1st_Earl_of_Leicester "Robert Dudley, 1st Earl of Leicester")) which were initially intended as a way of lifting the [siege of Antwerp](/wiki/Siege_of_Antwerp_(1584%E2%80%931585) "Siege of Antwerp (1584–1585)"), with an annual subsidy of 600,000 [florins](/wiki/Florin "Florin"), about a quarter of the annual cost of the revolt. As a surety for this assistance, the Dutch were to hand over [Brill](/wiki/Brielle "Brielle") and [Flushing](/wiki/Vlissingen "Vlissingen") to England, which it would garrison at its own expense. They were known as the [Cautionary Towns](/wiki/Cautionary_Towns "Cautionary Towns").[[1]](#cite_note-Wernham-1)

The treaty granted Elizabeth the right to appoint two councillors to the [Council of State of the United Provinces](/wiki/Council_of_State_of_the_Netherlands "Council of State of the Netherlands").

The surety provoked the objection of [Zeeland](/wiki/Zeeland "Zeeland"), which was to lose the most by this measure. Elizabeth rejected the title of General of the Provinces, offered to her in the treaty. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Aftermath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Treaty_of_Nonsuch&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Aftermath")]

Philip II viewed the treaty as a declaration of war against him by Elizabeth I, and the [Anglo-Spanish War](/wiki/Anglo-Spanish_War_(1585%E2%80%931604) "Anglo-Spanish War (1585–1604)") is conventionally considered to have begun at this point. Three years later, he launched the [Spanish Armada](/wiki/Spanish_Armada "Spanish Armada") and attempted to invade and conquer England. The resources spent by Philip on the Armada (10 million [ducats](/wiki/Ducat "Ducat")) undoubtedly diverted significant resources from fighting the [Dutch revolt](/wiki/Dutch_revolt "Dutch revolt"). Around 110 million ducats were spent on the partly-successful campaign against the resurgent revolt.

Meanwhile, Leicester's brief stint as governor of the United Provinces turned into a failure, as the Habsburg forces continued their advance, while Leicester came into increasing conflicts with the States of Holland and Zealand about their respective duties and competences. In October 1587, the States of Holland published the *Deduction* written by lawyer [François Vranck](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Vranck "François Vranck") (or Vrancken), arguing that the States had always held sovereignty over their province, and that no count, king, duke, prince, queen or earl could ever take a decision without their consideration and consent. [Johan van Oldenbarnevelt](/wiki/Johan_van_Oldenbarnevelt "Johan van Oldenbarnevelt"), backed by the States of Holland, revoked Leicester's command of the States' army at the end of 1587. In early April 1588, Leicester formally resigned as Governor of the United Provinces. Less than two weeks later, the States General issued the [instruction of 12 April 1588](/wiki/Instruction_of_12_April_1588 "Instruction of 12 April 1588"), which transferred all Leicester's duties and competences to the [Council of State](/wiki/Council_of_State_(Netherlands) "Council of State (Netherlands)"), while all officials were discharged of their oath to Leicester and given a new oath to the States General, marking the formal establishment of the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic"). Despite this fallout, the Treaty of Nonsuch remained in force, and the Anglo-Dutch alliance would defeat the Spanish Armada just a few months later in July–August 1588.

The Treaty of Nonsuch was renewed and amended by the Treaty of Westminster of 6/16 August 1598 between the States-General and the Privy Council on behalf of Elizabeth.[[2]](#cite_note-2)
